data.source: Laboratory/online behavioral experiments with human participants; computational simulations
data.wave_or_subset: Experiment 1: contingent-information vs full-information conditions; Experiment 2: contingent-information, full-information, individuation, occlusion, and noise intervention conditions
data.unit_of_analysis: Individual participant decisions/trials; model agent simulations
data.notes: Tasks used four binary feature dimensions determining outcomes. Experiment 1 used an approach-avoid task with cartoon bee stimuli. Experiment 2 used a job application evaluation task. Simulations included multiple feedback and attention configurations (e.g., contingent vs full-information, random-info, constructivist coding, no-attention).

method.description: The study combined computational simulations and human experiments to investigate attentional learning traps under different feedback regimes. The ALCOVE category-learning model was adapted to a reinforcement-learning setting (ALCOVE-RL) with selective attention and action-contingent feedback. Simulations manipulated feedback availability and attention mechanisms. Human Experiment 1 used an approach-avoid task (bees) under contingent vs full-information feedback to measure strategy use via 1D and 2D scores. Human Experiment 2 tested three interventions (individuation, occlusion, noise) in a job application task, tracking strategy adoption across learning blocks and a test phase. Bayesian multilevel models (estimated in Stan) analyzed continuous and binary behavioral measures.
steps: Adapt the ALCOVE model to ALCOVE-RL by incorporating action-dependent feedback and learnable attention weights.
Specify ALCOVE-RL parameters (c=6; τ=15; α_w=0.1; α_a=0.1) and use Luce's choice rule for probabilistic action selection.

Design a four-feature learning environment with payoffs determined by feature combinations; define feedback conditions (contingent, full-information, random-info) and attention variants (att vs no-att; constructivist coding).
Run simulations across conditions and measure learning speed, bias, and proportion of agents falling into learning traps or adopting the correct 2D rule.

Conduct Experiment 1 (N=101): assign participants to contingent or full-information feedback in a bee approach-avoid task; collect choices and compute 1D/2D strategy scores; compare conditions using Bayesian analyses.

Conduct Experiment 2 (N=400): assign participants to contingent, full-information, individuation, occlusion, or noise conditions in a job application task; track strategy use across eight learning blocks and a test phase; fit Bayesian multilevel models.
Evaluate intervention effectiveness on avoiding learning traps and on short- vs long-term learning performance.
method.models: ALCOVE-RL (similarity-based reinforcement learning with selective attention); Luce's choice rule; Bayesian multilevel models (normal for continuous, beta for binary measures) estimated in Stan
method.outcome_variable: Behavioral measures including approach/accept rates, one-dimensional (1D) strategy score, two-dimensional (2D) strategy score, proportion adopting correct 2D strategy, proportion falling into learning trap, explicit belief estimates
method.independent_variables: Feedback condition (contingent vs full-information vs random-info); attentional mechanism (attention vs no-attention; constructivist coding); intervention type (individuation, occlusion, noise); learning block/test phase
method.tools_software: Stan (four chains of 10,000 samples); custom computational simulations implementing ALCOVE-RL

results.summary: Across simulations and experiments, contingent (choice-dependent) feedback fostered attentional learning traps and biased one-dimensional strategies, whereas full-information feedback reduced bias and improved adoption of the correct two-dimensional strategy. In simulations, over a quarter of agents fell into traps under contingent feedback with attention, while full information or removing selective attention reduced traps and sped learning. Constructivist coding exacerbated traps. In Experiment 1, contingent participants sought more information early but converged on 1D strategies more than full-information participants; full-information participants showed higher 2D strategy use. In Experiment 2, contingent participants again showed greater trap tendencies, while interventions (individuation, occlusion, noise) had mixed effects: some reduced 1D reliance but also decreased 2D performance, with occlusion and noise initially slowing learning but preventing traps over time.
